items.myth: Variance means your features have large spread.
items.reality: In this topic, variance is Var(ŷ)—instability of model predictions across training samples—not Var(X) of input features.
items.example: Features scaled to zero mean and unit variance can still produce a high-variance classifier that memorizes labels.

items.myth: Overfitting is only about having too many features.
items.reality: Overfitting is high variance: the model fits sample-specific noise. Too-flexible model structure or too little data relative to capacity also causes it.
items.example: A single deep tree on 200 rows can overfit without hundreds of features—it splits on random noise in one column.

items.myth: If validation error is high, you always need a simpler model.
items.reality: High validation error may be high bias (underfitting) or high variance (overfitting). Check training error and learning curves before simplifying.
items.example: A linear model with 60% train and 58% val needs more capacity or features, not more regularization.

items.reality: CV estimates generalization error and helps choose models; it does not remove Var(ŷ) from the final model—you still need regularization, data, or ensembling.
items.example: Picking the best of 50 overfit models by CV still leaves a high-variance winner unless you constrain complexity.
